CargoLedger · Smart Invoicing Module

Two teams, one invoice, and an email chain that restarted every time one number was wrong.

A logistics company billed its clients through a spreadsheet-and-email relay between Operations and Accounts. A single mistyped container number bounced the whole file back to the start. I turned that relay into one shared, live invoice — cargo-accurate, tax auto-calculated, synced straight to the books.

RoleSenior UI/UX Designer
Duration10 weeks · 2026
ToolsFigma · FigJam · Prototyping
DomainB2B SaaS · Fintech · Logistics
INV · MOR2526SI01054
₹ 33,040
Suzlon Energy Pvt. Ltd.
Ready
7 × 20DV · 3 delivery orders₹ 28,000
CGST + SGST (18%)₹ 5,040
Surcharges₹ 0
Grand total₹ 33,040
Synced · Zoho
01 · Situation

Getting paid was a game of telephone.

When a job finished, the money hinged on one accurate invoice. But the record made a slow lap between two teams. One wrong field sent it back to the start. I mapped the loop before designing anything, because the delay wasn't a screen problem. It was a handoff problem.

Operations
Type into Excel
DO & container numbers, by hand.
→
Operations
Email the sheet
Spreadsheet sent to Accounts.
→
Accounts
Re-key & draft
Copy out, add GST & surcharges.
→
Accounts
Email back to check
Operations verifies the draft.
→
One wrong number
Rejected
Start over from scratch.

One wrong field killed the file

A missing container number or billing address bounced the whole invoice back. Massive delays and a mountain of billing errors, all from a single typo upstream.

Two teams, two languages

Operations thought in cargo — DOs, containers, addresses. Accounts thought in money — base amounts, GST, surcharges. The spreadsheet forced each to wade through the other's data to find its own.

02 · Research

Two teams who never needed the same information.

I mapped what each team actually used, and what they had to ignore. The gap between the two pointed straight at the fix: stop making them share a document, and start giving each the slice it needs off one record.

Upstream · Operations

Operations

Owns the cargo facts
  • Delivery orders, container codes, billing parties, drop-off addresses.
  • Keys those details in the moment a job is done.
  • Every typo here becomes a rejected invoice downstream.
"I just need to log the cargo once and move on."
Downstream · Accounts

Accounts

Turns cargo into money
  • Base amounts, GST, surcharges, totals.
  • Re-keys everything from the emailed sheet, then drafts.
  • Rejects the whole file if a single figure or reference is off.
"If the maths isn't transparent, I can't sign it off."
03 · The brief I set

One screen both teams could trust.

Kill the email-and-spreadsheet chain and replace it with a single module. I held myself to three rules, and judged every later decision against them.

Rule 01

One shared page

Both teams work from the exact same live record, at the same time. No files emailed back and forth, no version drift, no "which sheet is current?"

Rule 02

No information overload

Each team sees only what its job needs — cargo for Operations, money for Accounts — and nothing it has to scroll past.

Rule 03

Crystal-clear maths

Cost breakdowns detailed enough to trust at a glance, so mistakes get caught before they reach the customer, not after.

04 · Information architecture

One spine, two tabs.

Those three rules needed a shape. Pending Invoicing holds the work that owes money; Invoices holds the work that's been billed. Creation between them is a guarded path — with a preview you can't skip, because this is the step where money used to break.

Pending Invoicingunbilled by party
→
Add invoiceselect & charges
→
Choose formatsplit / consol / individual
→
Previewbefore commit
→
Invoicesstatus-tracked
→
Sync · Zohopush, retry on error
→
DeliverPDF to client
Invoiced Pending · to sync Ready Error · retry Cancelled
The Invoices tab · status-tracked, sync state per row, retry on error
05 · Wireframes

Structure first, in black and white.

I locked the skeleton in low fidelity before any colour, proving the flow while it was still cheap to change, and keeping the arguments about structure, not styling.

Invoice list — filters + status chip per row
Create invoice — bill-to + format presets
Invoice detail — charges + bill summary + total
Status & sync tracking — counters + per-row retry
06 · The core move

The same record, read two ways.

A single screen with every column would have drowned both teams: Operations scrolling past tax, Accounts digging for the money under cargo detail. So each team works in the screen its job needs. Operations logs cargo; Accounts reads money. Both run off one shared record, so the two can't drift out of sync.

One shared record · the screen each team actually works in
Operations works here: pick the finished jobs and confirm the cargo. No tax columns; not this team’s job.
Accounts works here: the same job read as money, every line opening into its own calculation.
07 · The shared overview

One live picture, not a spreadsheet hunt.

Both teams land on the same screen: what’s outstanding, what just moved, and where the revenue sits. I built it as a launchpad into the work, not a report to admire. One click from any row drops you into the full invoice.

The live overview · the screen both teams open first

One change I’d make in a v2: the four headline numbers are net revenue, MRR, active customers and outstanding. Two of them, MRR and active-customers, are subscription metrics, and this is a receivables desk, not a subscription business. I’d swap them for what Accounts actually chases: overdue aging and average days-to-pay. It’s the first thing I’d redraw.

08 · Operations

Confirm the cargo. The invoice drafts itself.

Operations picks the finished jobs and confirms the cargo facts. The moment that data is in, the invoice generates itself — no spreadsheet, no email, no waiting on Accounts to even start.

Pick jobs, choose the format · Split by DO
Preview · exactly what will be created, before commit
01

Cargo, not data entry

Jobs arrive pre-filled with their DO numbers, container codes and billing party. Operations confirms and selects — it never re-types what the system already knows, so the typo that used to sink the file has nowhere to enter.

The trade-offPre-filling moves the point of failure upstream: if the source data is wrong, confirming it launders the error instead of catching it. That is exactly why the preview step exists — a human still sees the finished invoice before anything commits.

02

Auto-drafted invoices

The moment cargo is confirmed, the invoice generates, mapping each line automatically instead of waiting on Accounts to key it in by hand.

The trade-offAuto-drafting can feel like the system took over. So nothing commits until preview and submit — an extra step I kept on purpose, because with money, one deliberate confirmation beats a fast mistake.

03

Grouped the way clients expect

Split by DO, Consolidated or Individual turns an error-prone judgement call into a labelled choice with a clear recommendation.

The trade-offI could have auto-picked the format from the data. I didn't — billing shape is a call clients care about, and hiding it in logic would trade a visible, correctable choice for an invisible one. So it's an explicit option with a sensible default, not a guess buried in code.

04

Preview before commit

Exactly which invoices will be created — every line, container and subtotal — is shown before anything is final. The draft Accounts used to assemble by hand now arrives ready to check.

05

Nothing final until it's chosen

"Modify selection" steps back without losing context, and nothing commits until submit. Because the flow handles money, I made it deliberately forgiving — both teams can explore formats and totals freely, knowing the old reject-and-restart loop is gone.

09 · Accounts

The maths, never a black box.

The same record, read for money: base amounts, GST, surcharges, totals. Every line opens into a step-by-step breakdown of how the cost was reached, so it can be trusted at a glance and signed off without a second spreadsheet. Click a line to see it work.

Invoice MOR2526SI01054 · chargesSuzlon Energy Pvt. Ltd.
Tariff per 20DV container₹ 4,000
Containers (across 3 DOs)× 7
Taxable subtotal₹ 28,000
CGST @ 9%₹ 2,520
SGST @ 9%₹ 2,520
Tax subtotal₹ 5,040
Grand total₹ 33,040

Transparency isn’t only in the breakdown. A tariff that deviates from the contracted rate flags itself inline, so a wrong number gets questioned before sign-off, not after the client disputes it.

Itemised & auditable

Every container, HSN code, tariff and tax line sits beside a bill summary and grand total — with "created by & on" always visible, so there's a clean trail behind every figure.

The trade-offFull itemisation makes the invoice long — a compact summary would fit on one screen. I chose density on purpose: for money, a figure you can trace beats a figure that fits, and the expandable rows keep the length from overwhelming.

Findable at scale

Search, filters and sorting keep thousands of invoices navigable; pagination keeps the table calm rather than endless.

The trade-offPaging keeps the table calm instead of infinite, but a bulk export needs its own route, so I kept bulk actions above the list, not buried inside a scroll.
Finding one invoice never means scrolling for it.

10 · Design system

Tuned for dense financial data.

A token-driven library built for tables, currency and status, so a thousand invoice rows stay calm and scannable. The one rule I held: status colour means the same thing in every place it appears, so the mental model never breaks between the donut, the pills and the rows.

11 · Result

Right the first time, and paid faster.

One synchronised screen replaced the loop of emailing spreadsheets, rejecting files and re-typing data. The back-and-forth simply stopped, and the money moved sooner.

−46%
fewer billing disputes from final clients. Automatic mapping and transparent breakdowns made internal errors vanish before they reached the customer.
Zero
email back-and-forth. Both teams log into one screen and see the same live record update instantly.
Instant
drafts — the invoice generates the moment Operations logs the cargo, instead of waiting on a re-key.
The number the business felt

Cash flow. An invoice that used to take days of relay now drafts in minutes and syncs straight to the books, so a finished job turns into billed revenue the same day, not after a week of email. The lever I'd track live is average days-to-pay: shorten the path from "fulfilled" to "invoiced" and the whole receivables cycle tightens behind it.

Sample customers, amounts and IDs shown throughout are illustrative. Result figures are from the project's own reported outcomes — happy to walk through how each was measured.

12 · What I'd do next

What I'd watch, and what I left out.

A finished screen isn't a finished product. Here's what I'd measure to be sure it worked, what I deliberately kept out of v1, and the one place I'd want more proof before trusting it at scale.

The number I'd track to prove it

The headline is fewer disputes, but the metric I'd watch live is the rejected-invoice rate and days-to-pay, before versus after. If "preview before commit" is really stopping errors — not just moving them downstream — that's where it shows.

What I scoped out of v1

I kept the first version to the core create → preview → sync loop for one currency and clean, fully-fulfilled jobs. Partial payments, credit notes and multi-currency are real, but each adds a branch to the money maths. I'd rather ship the spine solid than the whole tree shaky.

What I'd validate next

The three invoice formats — Split, Consolidated, Individual — covered every case I saw, but enterprise billing has a long tail. I'd test them against a wider client set before assuming three is enough.

The open risk

The Zoho sync is the one point where the flow leaves the product. A failed push needs a clean retry and honest error state. I built the states, but I'd want real error telemetry at volume before calling that path trustworthy.
